## [00:00 - 03:02]

Alright guys, so let's have a look at the pixel in a little bit more depth here. Now what you'll see in this section as well is there's also going to be a video on the basics of the pixel. I'm not covering that in this video in case I'm assuming that you're already across that. However, if you're brand new to Facebook ads, take a moment to go and watch that first. It just covers the basics of the Facebook pixel, how the code actually works, how it gets triggered, etc., etc. In this video, I just want to take you through essentially the tool box, the essential tool box for us as Facebook marketers when we're working with the pixel specifically in the context of e-commerce. Now as you guys already know, just as a quick recap for all of you guys, there's really three main reasons why you're going to be using the pixel, right? The first one is to track what you're doing with your ads, so track all of the conversions that are happening, the activities. Next you want to be able to get Facebook to optimize for the event or the trigger that you're actually trying to create such as purchases or add to carts. And then you want to be able to create custom audiences from people that visited the site, from people that added to cart, from people that triggered specific events, etc., etc., or even exclude those people from your campaigns. So those are really the three main reasons on why you're going to be using the pixel. Now being a great Facebook marketer is, as I've already mentioned before, 30% your skill set and expertise and 70% actually just understanding how to get out of the way and let Facebook optimize things for you. And the pixel is pretty much the key to all of that. Without a pixel in place, there's really not much in terms of, you know, the only campaigns you're really going to be running are things like lead ads, anything that takes place within Facebook or reach based campaigns, etc., etc. So it's really important to have that installed. Now the first thing that you guys really need in your toolbox, if you don't already have this, is the pixel tool helper installed on your Chrome plugin. Now you can just Google Facebook pixel helper and you'll see here it comes up and then you'll be able to add this to Chrome. Now what this does, if I pull up my own website here, is this is the little plugin already installed on my Chrome browser, is it actually pulls up the pixel information on all the websites. So you'll see here this is the pixel ID and then there's specific events. These are all standard Facebook ones, but if there was a purchase, etc., etc., you would see that here. Now one of the things that I really recommend guys is that you take the time to actually

## [03:02 - 06:02]

fully scope out your client's website front to back and double check using your pixel tool helper if there are any double ups in terms of the pixel. So double fire, so you would essentially see it firing off twice the same event. The reason I say this is that it's not uncommon for a lot of Shopify, especially plugins such as YachtPo, to potentially cause double reporting issues with pixels. So this is actually kind of a known thing within the community. What happens if you are not aware of this or if you don't address it or don't pick up on it is of course in your ad campaigns you're going to be thinking you're doing super well when in reality the client is seeing about half of the actual sales that are coming through. So it's not a great way to start with a new client campaign. Now the other tool that's really important here guys is actually your pixel tab. So if you come in here, this is business manager at the moment and you just click on pixels, you'll be able to preview pretty much all the hits that are happening within that pixel. So these ones here are the page views, so all the hits of the pixel. And then if we scroll down here you can see the specific either custom events or standard events that have been set up such as Hawaii Leads, FATC Purchase, this is a custom event that was created and you can see all of the triggers. And what this does is you can pretty much get a sense right away if there's something fundamentally wrong. So if you see a big giant dip somewhere, but overall when you're looking at this you can see okay it's got normal ups and downs, seems to be following the trend, etc. Now one of the things to be very clear about here guys is that this tab here, this is really important, is actually going to show reports on all triggers on that pixel regardless if it came from a Facebook ad or not. Meaning you could come down here and this is the period of a month here, so you could come down here and see FATC Purchase and go okay well there was 68 of them, but yet my ads only generated 40 for example. Why is Facebook telling me 68? Well the reason is that it was fired off within this time frame 68 times regardless if it came from an ad or not. So some of this would have come from maybe YouTube or organic traffic or emails that were being sent out, etc. etc. To actually see how many triggers, how many purchases if we're using this as an example

## [06:02 - 06:43]

came from ads, that's where you look at your reporting column under ads manager. So don't get a shock if you come in here and go well no I haven't had you know 68 sales for my ads or my client hasn't had 10 sales from our campaigns, there hasn't been 10 sales, where your client has had 10 sales but maybe they all came through organic or another means of traffic, which is that your campaigns haven't had that yet. So that's a really important thing. Now again if you need a recap on the basics of the pixel there's a video below that. In the next video I just want to take you guys quickly through custom events, how you set those up and then custom conversions as well.
